description: Showcase deck regenerated after removing the two subtitle-based multi-column layouts; now demonstrates the 22 supported production layouts with rounded inserted images except agenda icons.

slides.title: What This Showcase Validates
slides.body: This deck demonstrates the active SettleMint slide bank exactly as it exists today: one slide for each approved format, realistic DALP-focused placeholder content, conservative text fitting, and native PowerPoint output built through the current XML-merge workflow. The objective is not sales storytelling alone; it is proof that each template can be populated credibly without touching protected header, footer, numbering, or branded background zones.

slides.title: DALP makes asset design repeatable
slides.body:
• DALP gives issuers a controlled path from product design to servicing, with configurable asset templates, policy modules, identity checks, distribution rules, and settlement orchestration managed in one operating environment.
• Teams replace fragmented spreadsheets, custom scripts, and case-by-case legal interpretation with reusable workflows that product, compliance, operations, and engineering can govern together.
• The result is faster launch cycles, stronger auditability, clearer ownership across functions, and a delivery model suited to banks, market infrastructure providers, and enterprise tokenization programs.
• This showcase uses business-grade placeholder content to stress the layouts close to capacity while preserving readability and editability.

slides.title: Representative DALP capability matrix
slides.body: Illustrative capability table used to prove the table layout renders plausibly with dense but readable structured content.

slides.title: Delivery models by operating requirement
slides.body: The table compares how organizations typically stage production adoption. Pilot programs optimize for speed and learning. Controlled launches emphasize policy depth, operations readiness, and integration discipline. Enterprise scale focuses on repeatability across multiple products, jurisdictions, and internal teams. DALP is strongest where a client needs governance and reuse, not a one-off proof of concept.

slides.title: Value scales as controls become reusable
slides.body: A typical DALP journey starts with a single issuance use case, then compounds value as approved templates, compliance modules, identity checks, and downstream integrations are reused across new products. The chart area is intentionally populated after build with a visual asset so this layout demonstrates a fully occupied content zone rather than an empty placeholder.

slides.title: Why teams adopt DALP
slides.col1:
Bespoke build path
• **Specialist dependency stays high** because each launch reopens legal interpretation, contract setup, and operational design.
• Teams rebuild rules, data structures, and onboarding assumptions instead of reusing an approved operating pattern.
• Handoffs between product, compliance, and engineering create avoidable lag before anything reaches pilot readiness.
slides.col2:
DALP operating model
• **Governed templates shrink rework** by giving product, legal, and ops teams a shared configuration path.
• Eligibility checks, transfer controls, servicing logic, and evidence capture stay attached to the asset model.
• Integration points are explicit, so launch teams can move faster without losing control of downstream systems.

slides.title: Three core DALP pillars
slides.col1:
Design and issuance
• **Reusable parameters** reduce setup drift across products.
• Approval checkpoints stay visible before deployment.
• Launch teams move from concept to configured asset with less manual rework.
slides.col2:
Compliance and identity
• **Identity-backed rules** connect investor status to allowed actions.
• Enforcement remains visible, testable, and auditable.
• Manual screening bottlenecks shrink because policy logic is embedded early.
slides.col3:
Operations and integration
• **Event-led workflows** keep servicing steps consistent.
• APIs, monitoring, and downstream systems remain aligned to the same asset state.
• Growth does not require a fresh operating model for every new program.

slides.title: What enterprise buyers usually evaluate
slides.item1:
Control model
**One governed workflow** lets product, legal, compliance, and operations work in the same controlled lane instead of falling back to email approvals and spreadsheet reconciliation.
slides.item2:
Delivery speed
**Launch-ready configuration** shortens time to pilot while preserving review gates, evidence capture, and downstream integration quality for each product.
slides.item3:
Runtime enforcement
**Rules execute systematically** so transfer, holding, onboarding, and servicing controls do not depend on manual interpretation once the asset is live.
slides.item4:
Scale potential
**The operating model expands cleanly** across new products, jurisdictions, and client programs without forcing a redesign every time demand grows.

slides.title: Six design principles behind production readiness
slides.item1:
Reusable templates
**Standardize issuer setup** so launch teams reuse approved patterns, shorten review loops, and avoid rebuilding controls for every product.
slides.item2:
Policy first
**Expose constraints early** so approvals, jurisdiction choices, and investor rules are visible before an asset reaches deployment.
slides.item3:
Identity linked
**Bind status to action** so verified investor attributes drive who can hold, receive, or service the instrument in production.
slides.item4:
Event driven
**Keep systems synchronized** through explicit state changes, servicing events, and operational signals instead of ad hoc follow-up work.
slides.item5:
Operational evidence
**Leave an audit trail** with reviewable records, approval context, and policy outcomes that support governance and regulator questions.
slides.item6:
Human editable
**Keep decks and outputs usable** by giving teams native, editable content rather than brittle screenshots or flattened explanations.

slides.title: Asset design workflow in practice
slides.body: The asset designer experience gives product and operations teams a guided path from instrument setup to policy configuration and review. **The strongest benefit is operational clarity:** each step makes the next decision explicit, which helps teams explain why a product is ready, what still needs approval, and how configuration choices connect to launch controls. This layout shows that detailed narrative can stay readable while using the available text area well.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/03 - Asset Designer/Asset Designer Review and Deploy 1.jpg

slides.title: Compliance policies stay close to execution
slides.body: DALP is strongest when compliance is not treated as a document set that lives outside the product workflow. **Policy logic stays close to execution:** templates, eligibility expressions, and review controls become part of the operating model itself. That reduces handoffs, improves auditability, and makes it easier for teams to explain how regulated behavior will hold up once the asset moves from pilot to scaled operations.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/11 - Compliance and Policy Templates/Policy Templates - List.jpg

slides.title: Executive conversations need business context
slides.body: When the audience is commercial, strategy, or transformation leadership, visuals do not need to be product screenshots only. A balanced image-plus-text layout can frame the boardroom problem: institutions want tokenization programs that reduce launch friction, keep control functions comfortable, and create a repeatable path to additional products. This format gives room for narrative without looking text-heavy. **This format works best when the visual proves credibility** and the text explains operating implications, rollout choices, and stakeholder ownership in plain language.

slides.title: Monitoring and operations matter after launch
slides.body: Production readiness is not proven at issuance alone. Teams also need visibility into requests, events, servicing activity, and exceptions once products are live. This slide is designed to communicate that the operating model extends beyond setup screens: monitoring and auditability are part of what makes a platform credible in enterprise environments. **It is useful for executive storytelling** because the visual holds attention while the narrative can still clarify controls, dependencies, and expected outcomes.

slides.title: Real-estate assets need governed workflows
slides.body: Wide visual layouts work well when the asset itself is tangible and context-rich. For real-estate tokenization, DALP helps structure the asset record, investor constraints, servicing events, and downstream reporting around one controlled model rather than a patchwork of custom handling.

slides.title: Identity-led onboarding supports cleaner distribution
slides.body: A narrow text area forces prioritization: identity, eligibility, and verification need to be clear, not verbose. This layout is effective when the message is that token distribution quality depends on who is allowed to hold, transfer, and service the instrument, not just on how quickly tokens can be minted.

slides.title: Technology positioning can stay business-readable
slides.body: One-third image layouts work well when the visual is supportive rather than dominant. **The text still carries the business message:** DALP gives institutions a structured way to coordinate product design, compliance choices, operational evidence, and system integration without turning every launch into a bespoke transformation project. The narrower text area is now judged against its real geometry so the content is neither visibly sparse nor pushed into unreadable overflow. Add rollout sequencing, stakeholder accountability, and integration expectations, and the slide still remains comfortably readable. **That extra density is intentional:** the builder now treats this as a narrow narrative box, not a generic body area.

slides.title: Deployment choices should reflect governance maturity
slides.body: Clients usually move through phases: prove the use case, assign control ownership, integrate target systems, then scale. **That progression needs disciplined narrative space** because each phase has different governance questions, approval thresholds, and operating risks. This narrow-body layout now gets fit-scored against the actual placeholder box, which helps the builder keep the explanation dense enough to feel intentional without making the slide hard to read. It can now hold the practical detail that buyers usually ask for: who signs off, what integrates next, and where control evidence will come from. **That matters for proposal work,** where compressed business guidance still has to feel complete and boardroom-ready.

slides.title: SettleMint pairs platform depth with discipline
slides.body: Full-width visual slides are best when the audience needs a momentum moment rather than another dense content block. This one reinforces the idea that DALP is not simply a feature set; it is a disciplined operating model for institutions that want to move from isolated experiments to governed, repeatable digital-asset delivery.
